top_requirements.requirement: Cross-functional collaboration with Product/Design (and often Data Science/QA)
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 141 of 212 postings explicitly named working with product managers/designers

top_requirements.requirement: X+ years of professional mobile experience (most commonly 3-5 for 'senior', 5-8 for staff)
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 131 of 212 postings stated an explicit years-of-experience bar

top_requirements.requirement: REST/GraphQL API integration with backend services

top_requirements.approx_frequency: 121 of 212 postings (roughly matches the ~half of the corpus that is Android-flavored)

top_requirements.requirement: Mentoring / raising the technical bar for other engineers
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 105 of 212 postings, concentrated at Senior/Staff titles -- framed as an incidental senior-level duty, not a primary people-management mandate

top_requirements.requirement: CI/CD pipeline familiarity (build, test, and release automation)
Comfort with or active use of AI coding tools day-to-day (Copilot/Cursor/'AI-assisted', explicitly as an engineering workflow expectation, not a product feature)
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 31 of 212 postings named this specifically; a much larger 146 of 212 mention 'AI' somewhere in the text but most of that is AI as a product feature (the app itself does something AI-related), not a coding-tool requirement

top_requirements.requirement: App Store / Play Store release process fluency (submission, staged rollout, review process)
Architecture pattern fluency: MVVM / MVP / VIPER / Clean Architecture
Jetpack Compose (Android) / SwiftUI (iOS) as the modern UI toolkit, often alongside legacy UIKit/Views expectation
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 58 of 212 (Compose) and 57 of 212 (SwiftUI), each roughly half of their respective platform's postings, signaling the modern-toolkit transition is well underway but not fully complete -- many postings still ask for UIKit (34) or Java (61) fluency too
52 of 212 postings, concentrated in growth-stage/consumer companies rather than fintech/big tech

top_requirements.requirement: Unit/UI testing discipline (XCTest, Espresso, or general 'unit test' language)
Security-conscious development (secure API/auth/data handling, not adversarial red-teaming)
Bachelor's degree named as a general qualification
top_requirements.approx_frequency: 27 of 212 postings; a strict 'degree required' framing (vs. 'or equivalent experience') appeared in only 2 of 212, confirming degree requirements are usually soft
24 of 212 postings (broader phrase-matching including generic 'accessible' language put this as high as 65 of 212)

top_responsibilities: Translate design specs/product requirements into shipped, production-quality screens and features on the native platform (Swift/SwiftUI or Kotlin/Compose) or a shared cross-platform codebase (React Native), owning the work end-to-end from implementation through release.

Partner closely and continuously with Product Managers, Designers, and (at larger companies) Data Science/QA as a standing cross-functional squad -- feasibility conversations and UX tradeoffs are a routine, not occasional, part of the week.

Own performance and resource-budget work specific to constrained devices -- app startup/cold-start time, memory footprint, battery drain, frame-rate/jank -- described across a very wide swath of postings (roughly 150 of 212 once phrased broadly) as an explicit, monitored engineering concern rather than a one-off.

Participate in code review and, at Senior/Staff level, mentor less-experienced engineers and help set team-wide coding/architecture standards -- this is the single most common 'beyond your own tickets' duty in the corpus.

Manage the platform-specific release lifecycle: App Store/Play Store submission and review, staged/phased rollouts, crash-reporting triage (fewer explicit named tools than expected -- Crashlytics/Sentry named in only 10 of 212 -- but 'crash-free' and release-quality metrics referenced far more often), and rollback/kill-switch planning since a shipped binary can't be hot-patched like a server.

At Staff+, drive cross-team technical direction: architecture/design reviews 'across team boundaries,' modularization, build-tooling and developer-productivity investment, and setting standards consumed by multiple pods rather than owning a single feature (seen explicitly at Block/Cash App, Airwallex, Airbnb, and OpenSea staff-level postings).

Instrument and act on production/business metrics tied to the shipped app -- crash-free sessions, retention, conversion/engagement, KPIs/OKRs -- appearing in a substantial minority (92 of 212) of postings, more common at consumer/growth-stage and fintech companies than at big-tech platform-team roles.

A smaller but real subset of postings (roughly 15%) frame the mobile engineer as expected to actively use AI coding tools (Copilot/Cursor/'AI-assisted' workflows) as part of the daily engineering loop, not just tolerate them -- this is a 2025-2026-era addition that older mobile-engineer job description templates would not reflect.

comp_signals.range_text: Tiered by region: $142,000-$178,000 / $130,000-$163,000 / $120,000-$150,000 (iOS Engineer); $162,000-$203,000 range for Senior iOS Engineer
$105,000-$145,000 (Android Engineer) up to $170,000-$230,000 (Staff iOS Engineer, Connectivity) across multiple postings
Region-banded: e.g. $168,100-$227,400 (San Francisco), up to $261,500 (New York) for Senior iOS Engineer
$125,000 - $265,000 (Senior Android Software Engineer, Platform); $147,000 - $265,000 (Android Software Engineer, Applications)
$190,000 to $280,000, with a $220,000-$260,000 sub-band called out (Staff iOS Engineer, AI Native, Family AI Lab)

seniority_distribution_notes: Skews senior: of 212 titles, 82 explicitly say "Senior," 28 say "Staff," 11 say "Lead," and 3 say "Principal" -- roughly 60% of the corpus is Senior-or-above by title alone. Only 2 titles read as entry-level ("Engineer I" / roman numeral I), and 16 are the "II" mid-level band (mostly at WHOOP, which titles consistently by band). 70 titles carry no seniority qualifier at all (bare "Mobile Engineer," "Android Engineer," "iOS Engineer") -- reading their body text, most of these still specify multi-year experience bars (3-6 years) in the requirements section, so true green-field/junior openings are rare in this sample. This matches the existing brief's implicit assumption of a senior-leaning market but is worth flagging explicitly: the live market is not evenly distributed across the ladder, and a mobile-engineer archetype match for a junior-profile user should expect a thinner set of open reqs than a senior-profile user.
notable_divergence_from_existing_brief: Mostly confirms the brief, with two refinements worth flagging. (1) The brief's "18 postings" era text undersells how senior-skewed and mentoring-heavy the market is: mentoring/bar-raising language appears in half the corpus (105/212) concentrated at Senior+, which is stronger and more explicit evidence for the brief's people_management_orientation-adjacent framing than the original small sample showed. (2) AI-tooling-as-expected-workflow is real but should not be overstated: only ~31/212 postings (about 15%) explicitly expect daily AI-coding-tool usage as a job requirement, even though "AI" as a keyword appears in 146/212 -- the large majority of those AI mentions are about building AI-powered app features (the product), not about the engineer's own workflow. The brief's Life360/Speechify-anchored narrative about "AI-native" mobile work is a real and growing pattern but is a minority signal, not yet the market center of gravity, and results copy should avoid implying it's now-typical. Everything else -- comp structure (base+equity+bonus, no distinct variable component), cross-functional Product/Design collaboration as the dominant non-coding activity, App Store/Play Store release-cycle friction, and performance-budget work as a named engineering concern -- is strongly corroborated by this larger sample.

dimension_evidence.note: 113 of 212 postings use ambiguity/ownership language ('fast-paced,' 'move fast,' 'autonomy,' 'limited guidance'), but this is nearly always paired with a design spec or product brief as the starting point rather than fully undefined problem space -- consistent with a mid target rather than a high one.

No posting frames the role as inbound-queue/ticket-driven; work is described as sustained feature-building punctuated by scheduled code review and release cycles, not ad hoc interruptions. Low target holds.

Only 15 of 212 postings mention on-call/incident/pager language explicitly, and named crash-tooling (Crashlytics/Sentry) appears in just 10 -- this is real but a minority, secondary duty in the sample, matching a mid (not high) target.

Performance/battery/memory/jank language is very common (150 of 212 on a broad match), consistent with debugging that regularly crosses the app-code/OS-device boundary, matching the anchor-3 description.

65 of 212 postings reference platform/cross-team scope, but on inspection this concentrates almost entirely in Staff-level titles (28 of 212) describing architecture ownership bounded to one app or one platform's engineering org (e.g. 'across the iOS stack,' 'Android community at Cash App') rather than a platform serving many unrelated consuming teams. Low-moderate target holds.

Requirements stay concentrated within one platform layer (Swift/Kotlin/RN) even at Staff level; xAI's Rust-for-backend ask is a notable outlier, not the norm. Supports keeping the target low (specialist-leaning).
92 of 212 postings name production/business metrics (crash-free rate, retention, conversion, KPIs/OKRs) tied to the shipped app, a real but not universal pattern -- mid target is well-supported.

Only 14 of 212 postings use client-facing/customer-facing/enterprise-customer language; the dominant cross-functional pattern is internal Product/Design collaboration, not external stakeholder management. Low-moderate target holds.

Only 9 of 212 postings mention anything resembling public teaching/conference/open-source content; internal code-review mentoring (105 of 212) is a distinct, more common pattern already captured under people_management_orientation, not this dimension. Low target holds.
No posting in the sample frames work around a portfolio of named client/account relationships; work organizes around app/feature/platform ownership throughout. Low target holds.
Same evidence as account_portfolio_breadth -- no named-relationship framing anywhere in the sample.

All 18 postings with explicit dollar figures describe flat base-salary bands with no commission/variable-comp language; equity/bonus mentioned generically alongside base, matching a standard SWE comp shape. Low target holds.
Building/shipping production UI and business logic is the dominant activity described in essentially every posting at every level sampled, including Staff. Max target strongly supported.
No posting in the sample mentions client-site travel or physical embedding; in-office expectations (e.g. Robinhood's 3 days/week) are about a home office, not travel. Low target holds.

39 of 212 postings mention security, but reading context this is almost entirely secure-coding/compliance language (auth, data handling, App Store review policy) rather than adversarial red-team thinking. Low-moderate target holds.

Battery/memory/startup/frame-rate language is very common (150 of 212 broad match) and described with real budget/CI-gating framing at a subset of companies (Oura, WHOOP, wearables-adjacent firms most explicitly, given the BLE/hardware-adjacent context), supporting a real-but-soft (not hard pass/fail) resource-budget framing at target 3.

Mentoring is very common (105 of 212) but consistently framed as raising the technical bar / growing engineers alongside one's own IC work, not a primary growth-and-retention people-management mandate -- mid-low target with incidental framing holds.
No posting in the sample names public speaking, published content, or social community engagement as a job expectation. Low target holds.

AI is frequently mentioned (146 of 212) but almost entirely as a product feature the app surfaces, not as a requirement to build/train/productionize ML systems -- ML engineering fluency proper is out of scope. Low target holds.

Trivially and universally confirmed -- native/cross-platform mobile skill (Swift 108/212, Kotlin 121/212, React Native 52/212, Flutter 20/212) is the literal subject-matter gate of every posting in the corpus.
No posting in the sample asks for data pipeline/warehouse/ETL ownership; API consumption (123 of 212) is the closest adjacent skill and is a distinct, already-captured pattern. Low target holds.
CI/CD familiarity is common (87 of 212) but framed as using build/release pipelines for the app, not building or operating cloud infrastructure/internal developer platforms. Low target holds.

representative_companies_currently_hirin.title: Staff Android Software Engineer, Cash App Consumer Platform
